title: Localization Transitions in a Half-Filled Helical Aubry-André Model
authors: Taylan Yildiz, B. Tanatar, Balázs Hetényi
abstract: We study localization in a one-dimensional quasiperiodic lattice obtained by extending the Aubry-André model with an additional $N$th-neighbor hopping term of strength $J_{N}$. This long-range tunneling couples successive windings of an effective helical chain and introduces a second control parameter beyond the quasiperiodic potential strength $Δ$. Working with noninteracting fermions (typically at half filling), we diagnose the delocalization-localization transition using extensions of the modern theory of polarization. Specifically, we compute the polarization amplitudes of the many-body Slater-determinant ground state and construct a geometric Binder cumulant from polarization amplitudes. The critical potential where the localization transition happens is extracted from the sign change (zero crossing) of the geometric Binder cumulant. We map critical potential as a function of $J_N$ and the helical range $N$, finding that stronger helical hopping generally stabilizes the extended phase (shifting critical potential upward), while the $N$-dependence can display pronounced commensurability-induced spikes. We further compare the geometric Binder cumulant with the Fermi gap, which remains near zero at small values of potential and opens in the same parameter regime where the geometric Binder cumulant departs from extended phase. Finally, to take a controlled thermodynamic limit along Fibonacci system sizes, we employ a Zeckendorf-shift construction that fixes the many-body sector consistently as system size goes to infinity.
summary: This paper explores how long-range, helical connections modify the localization transition in a quasiperiodic system. It reveals that helical hopping stabilizes the delocalized phase and that the transition point fluctuates significantly due to resonance effects between the hopping range and the lattice potential.
main_problem: The study investigates how long-range helical hopping and the winding range N influence the delocalization-localization transition in a 1D quasiperiodic lattice.
main_result: Increasing helical hopping strength stabilizes the extended phase, and the critical potential exhibits non-monotonic spikes due to resonant helical sampling of the quasiperiodic landscape.
method: The authors use the modern theory of polarization to construct a geometric Binder cumulant and employ a Zeckendorf-shift construction with Fibonacci system sizes to perform controlled thermodynamic limit extrapolations.
model_or_system: A one-dimensional helical Aubry-André model featuring an incommensurate onsite potential and an Nth-neighbor hopping term that connects successive windings of a helical chain.
key_observables: Geometric Binder cumulant (U4), polarization amplitudes (Zq), normalized Fermi gap, and single-particle energy spectrum.
important_parameters: Quasiperiodic potential strength (Delta), Nth-neighbor hopping strength (JN), helical range (N), and system size (L).
main_assumptions: The study focuses on non-interacting fermions at half-filling and utilizes periodic boundary conditions.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the helical lattice geometry; Figure 6 displays the Binder cumulant and Fermi gap for different hopping strengths; Figure 7 illustrates the energy spectrum changes; Figure 8 shows the Binder cumulant for various Fibonacci sizes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the helical Aubry-André model, details the polarization-based diagnostic methods and thermodynamic limit construction, presents results on phase stabilization and commensurability-induced spikes, and concludes with a robustness analysis via finite-size scaling.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Schematic illustration of a one-dimensional lattice embedded on a toroidal geometry. The sites follow a helical path wrapped around the torus, such that periodic boundary conditions are naturally realized by the closed topology. The shaded region highlights a local segment of the lattice. (b) Effective representation of the helical structure, where sites on neighboring turns become spatially close. Solid lines de- note nearest-neighbor hopping J, while dotted links indicate longer-range hopping JN emerging from the geometric em- bedding.

FIG. 2. Geometric Binder cumulant U4 as a function of quasiperiodic potential strength ∆for L = 987 and N = 40, shown for several Nth-neighbor hopping amplitudes JN (panel labels). Horizontal dotted lines indicate U4 = 1/3, 1/2 and U4 = 0. The vertical dotted line marks the critical po- tential ∆c defined by the zero crossing of U4(∆).

FIG. 4. Critical quasiperiodic potential strength ∆c as a func- tion of the long-range (helical) hopping amplitude JN for a chain of length L = 987 at half filling. The critical value ∆c is extracted from the zero crossing of the geometric Binder cumulant U4(∆) upon increasing ∆, with the helical hopping range fixed to (a) N = 100 (b)N = 40 and JN varied linearly from 0 to 1.

FIG. 5. Dependence of the critical quasiperiodic potential strength ∆c on the helical winding (long-range hopping range) N for a chain of length L = 987 at half filling, where ∆c is extracted from the zero crossing of the geometric Binder cumulant. Left panel: JN = 0.5; right panel: JN = 1.0. Some pronounced spikes occur at N values with gcd(N, L) > 1the , inset graph corresponds to gcd(N, L) vs N.

FIG. 3. Geometric Binder cumulant U4 as a function of quasiperiodic potential strength ∆for L = 987 and N = 100, shown for several Nth-neighbor hopping amplitudes JN (panel labels). Horizontal dotted lines indicate U4 = 1/3, 1/2 and U4 = 0. The vertical dotted line marks the critical po- tential ∆c defined by the zero crossing of U4(∆).
